lookup_context: Departure aircraft is taxiing to the assigned runway
The taxi route includes another runway before the departure runway
The runway geometry is the issue shown in the runway/taxiway proximity figures
items.task: Decide how the crossing affects takeoff clearance.
items.instruction: Use the runway/taxiway proximity figures with the takeoff clearance rule.
items.question_text: Before issuing takeoff clearance, what must normally be true about runways along the taxi route to the departure runway?

choices.text: They must already have been crossed, unless the specific exception requiring crossing phraseology applies.
They may remain uncrossed as long as the aircraft is holding short of the departure runway.
They may be crossed after takeoff clearance without being mentioned.
They only matter if another aircraft is on final for the crossed runway.

items.explanation: The figures support the rule that runways along the taxi route to the departure runway are crossed before takeoff clearance, unless configuration/marking limitations require the crossed runway to be stated with the clearance.

lookup_context: Helicopter or powered-lift aircraft must move above the surface
Controller is choosing between hover-taxi and air-taxi phraseology
The controlling distinction is speed/height above the surface
items.instruction: Use the taxi options table implied by the paragraph.
items.question_text: Which option matches a slow movement above the surface, normally below 20 knots and in ground effect?

choices.text: Hover-taxi.
Air-taxi.
Ground taxi.
Simultaneous takeoff.

items.explanation: Hover-taxi is the slow movement above the surface, normally below 20 knots and in ground effect. Air-taxi is normally below 100 feet AGL and above 20 knots.

lookup_context: Tower issues AIR-TAXI instructions
No different altitude is requested or instructed
Controller needs the operating expectation from the taxi options table
items.instruction: Use the taxi options table implied by the paragraph.
items.question_text: Unless otherwise requested or instructed, what does an air-taxi authorization normally imply?

choices.text: The pilot is expected to remain below 100 feet AGL and is responsible for selecting a safe airspeed.
The aircraft must stay in ground effect below 20 knots.
The aircraft must remain on the paved taxiway surface.
The aircraft is cleared to climb to pattern altitude.

items.explanation: Air-taxi authorizes movement above the surface, normally below 100 feet AGL and above 20 knots unless otherwise requested or instructed. The pilot remains responsible for safe airspeed.

lookup_context: Aircraft is on vertically guided final
Aircraft is within 2 miles of the runway threshold
Official ceiling is below 300 feet or visibility is less than 3/4 SM
items.instruction: Use the POFZ figure and weather triggers.
items.question_text: What does this figure-based condition require?

choices.text: Keep the POFZ clear when the vertically guided final aircraft is within 2 miles and the weather trigger is met.
Apply the POFZ only after the aircraft crosses the threshold.
Ignore the POFZ if the aircraft is already cleared to land.
Apply the POFZ only to departing aircraft.

items.explanation: The POFZ restriction is triggered by an aircraft on vertically guided final within 2 miles when ceiling is below 300 feet or visibility is less than 3/4 SM.

lookup_context: Two aircraft will operate from runways in the same direction
Controller is evaluating whether the simultaneous same direction layout applies
The runway geometry is the key fact
items.task: Identify the correct use of the figure.
items.instruction: Use the simultaneous same direction operation figure.
items.question_text: What is the figure primarily helping the controller determine?

choices.text: Whether the runway geometry supports simultaneous same direction operations under the applicable rule.
Whether light gun signals are required.
Whether an aircraft is eligible for air-taxi.
Whether wake turbulence categories can be ignored.

items.explanation: The same direction figure is a geometry aid for applying the simultaneous same direction runway operation rule.

lookup_context: Two aircraft will operate from runways in opposite directions
Controller is evaluating whether simultaneous opposite direction operation applies
The runway geometry is the key fact
items.task: Identify the correct use of the figure.
items.instruction: Use the simultaneous opposite direction operation figure.
items.question_text: What is the figure primarily helping the controller determine?

choices.text: Whether the runway geometry supports simultaneous opposite direction operations under the applicable rule.
Whether beacon-code assignment is correct.
Whether runway edge lights should be set to step 3.
Whether aircraft identification may be abbreviated.

items.explanation: The opposite direction figure is a geometry aid for applying the simultaneous opposite direction runway operation rule.

lookup_context: Publication Schedule table
Row is Change 2 under JO 7110.65CC cycle
Controller is checking the effective publication date
items.task: Read the effective date from the table.
items.instruction: Use the publication schedule table.
items.question_text: What is the effective date for Change 2 in the JO 7110.65CC cycle?
items.explanation: TBL 1-1-1 lists Change 2 in the JO 7110.65CC cycle with cutoff 6/10/27 and effective date 11/25/27.

lookup_context: Military Distribution Contacts table
Facility is U.S. Air Force
Order or change has not arrived at least 30 days before effective date
items.instruction: Use the military distribution contacts table.
items.question_text: Which headquarters contact applies for the U.S. Air Force?

choices.text: HQ AFFSA.
USAASA.
CNO (N980A).
Service area office distribution officer.

items.explanation: TBL 1-1-2 lists HQ AFFSA as the U.S. Air Force military headquarters contact.

lookup_context: Military Operations Interface Offices table
Procedure deviation involves Department of the Army operations
Controller needs the appropriate interface office
items.instruction: Use the military operations interface offices table.
items.question_text: Which office is listed for Department of the Army interface?

choices.text: Director, USAASA (MOAS-AS).
Chief of Naval Operations, N980A, NAATSEA.
HQ AFFSA.
FAA service area office distribution officer.

items.explanation: TBL 1-1-3 lists Director, USAASA (MOAS-AS), for Department of the Army interface.

lookup_context: Controller is applying order-wide word meanings
The figure supports interpreting divergence and directional terms
Question concerns the default meaning of directions and miles
items.instruction: Use the word-meanings figure and terminology list.
items.question_text: Which interpretation is correct unless the order specifically says otherwise?

choices.text: Directions such as course, bearing, azimuth, heading, and wind direction are magnetic; miles are nautical except visibility uses statute miles.
All directions are true and all miles are statute miles.
Wind direction is true, but course and heading are magnetic.
Visibility and distance are both always nautical miles.

items.explanation: Paragraph 1-2-1 defines the default terminology: listed directional terms are magnetic unless specifically stated otherwise, and miles are nautical except visibility.

lookup_context: FAA Order JO 7110.65 abbreviations table
Controller is reading a shorthand entry
Entry is APREQ
items.instruction: Use the abbreviations table.
items.question_text: What does APREQ mean in the table?

choices.text: Approval Request.
Airport arrival rate.
ATC preferred route.
Adapted departure route.

items.explanation: TBL 1-2-1 lists APREQ as Approval Request.

lookup_context: Flight progress strip entry must be hand printed
Controller is preventing misinterpretation
Standard hand-printed characters figure applies
items.instruction: Use the standard hand-printed characters figure.
items.question_text: Why does the paragraph direct controllers to use standard hand-printed characters?

choices.text: To prevent misinterpretation of current data and clearances posted on flight progress strips.
To make strip entries match computer keyboard fonts.
To replace the requirement to post current strip data.
To allow erased entries to remain readable.

items.explanation: FIG 2-3-1 supports the requirement to use standard hand-printed characters so strip data is not misinterpreted.

lookup_context: Aircraft Equipment Suffixes table
Navigation capability is any
Transponder is failed
items.instruction: Use the aircraft equipment suffixes table.
items.question_text: Which equipment suffix applies?
items.explanation: TBL 2-3-10 assigns /H when navigation capability is any and the transponder has failed.

lookup_context: ICAO phonetics table
Controller is pronouncing an individual number
Character is 9
items.instruction: Use the ICAO phonetics table.
items.question_text: How is the number 9 pronounced?

choices.text: NIN-ER.
NINE.
NIN-AH.
NIN-TEE.

items.explanation: TBL 2-4-1 gives the pronunciation of 9 as NIN-ER.

lookup_context: Two aircraft have similar sounding identifications
Controller is replying on frequency
Aircraft identification table applies
items.instruction: Use the aircraft identification table.
items.question_text: How should the controller reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications?

choices.text: Use the full identification.
Use only the final three characters.
Use the aircraft type without the registration.
Use abbreviated identification until challenged.

items.explanation: The paragraph directs controllers to use full identification in reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications.

lookup_context: ATC light signals table
Signal is flashing red
Aircraft is in flight
items.instruction: Use the ATC light signals table.
items.question_text: What does flashing red mean to an aircraft in flight?

choices.text: Airport unsafe, do not land.
Cleared to land.
Return for landing.
Give way to other aircraft and continue circling.

items.explanation: TBL 3-2-1 lists flashing red to aircraft in flight as airport unsafe, do not land.

lookup_context: REIL three-step intensity table
Night operation
Visibility is 2 miles
items.instruction: Use the REIL intensity table.
items.question_text: Which REIL setting applies at night with 2 miles visibility?

choices.text: Setting 2.
Setting 3.
Setting 1.
Off unless requested.

items.explanation: TBL 3-4-1 assigns REIL setting 2 at night for visibility from 1 to but not including 3 miles.

lookup_context: PAPI five-step intensity table
Remote intensity control is available
Operation is from sunset to sunrise
items.instruction: Use the PAPI intensity table.
items.question_text: Which PAPI step applies from sunset to sunrise?

choices.text: Step 3.
Step 4.
Step 5.
Step 1.

items.explanation: TBL 3-4-4 assigns PAPI step 3 from sunset to sunrise.

lookup_context: MIRL intensity table
Day operation
Visibility is less than 2 miles
items.instruction: Use the MIRL intensity table.
items.question_text: Which MIRL step applies during the day with visibility less than 2 miles?

choices.text: Step 3.
Step 2.
Step 1.
Off unless requested.

items.explanation: TBL 3-4-10 assigns MIRL step 3 during the day when visibility is less than 2 miles.

lookup_context: Aircraft will conduct an overhead maneuver
Aircraft reaches the initial point depicted by the figure
Flight is on an IFR flight plan
items.instruction: Use the overhead maneuver figure.
items.question_text: What happens to the IFR flight plan when the aircraft reaches initial?

choices.text: The IFR flight plan is canceled.
The IFR flight plan remains active until touchdown.
The IFR flight plan is canceled only after the break.
The IFR flight plan remains active until the aircraft exits the runway.

items.explanation: For overhead maneuvers, the IFR flight plan is canceled when the aircraft reaches the initial point.

lookup_context: Unmanned free balloon is within controlled airspace
The figure shows that balloon shape varies at different altitudes
Controller is considering whether to use vertical separation
items.task: Apply the balloon figure and separation caution.
items.instruction: Use the free-balloon figure with the separation rule.
items.question_text: When may vertical separation be used with an unmanned free balloon?

choices.text: Only when accurate balloon altitude information is available.
Whenever the balloon's general direction is known.
Whenever the operator gave one hour advance notice of descent.
Whenever the payload is expected to descend by parachute.

items.explanation: The balloon figure reinforces that free balloons change shape and altitude profile; vertical separation is not used unless accurate balloon altitude information is available.
